summary:
Hugo Grotius's The Law of War and Peace (De Jure Belli ac Pacis, 1625 AD) was one of the foundational texts of international law — the systematic elaboration of principles governing the relations between states, the conduct of war, and the rights of neutral parties that established Grotius as the 'father of international law.' Grotius, a Dutch jurist, diplomat, and polymath of the [[event:2326|Dutch Golden Age]], had already laid groundwork with Mare Liberum (The Free Sea, 1609), which argued for freedom of navigation on the high seas — a position serving Dutch commercial interests against Portuguese claims to exclusive oceanic control. De Jure Belli ac Pacis, written while Grotius was in exile in France after escaping imprisonment in the Netherlands, expanded these principles into a comprehensive theory of the law of nations.

Grotius drew on classical sources, scripture, and natural reason to argue that there existed a binding law of nations — not merely based on the will of any sovereign or the authority of the Church, but grounded in reason and natural law that all states were obligated to observe. His elaboration of 'just war' doctrine, the rights of ambassadors, the treatment of prisoners and civilian populations, and the conditions under which neutrality must be respected influenced the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]] negotiations (1648) and the entire subsequent tradition of international law. While Grotius's specific arguments have been superseded, his project of constructing a secular, rational framework for relations between sovereign states established the conceptual foundations on which modern international law — from the Geneva Conventions to the United Nations Charter — is built.
